title: Enhancing Goodput in Large-Scale LLM Training with Nonuniform Tensor Parallelism | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA introduces Nonuniform Tensor Parallelism (NTP), an experimental framework that dynamically lowers the tensor-parallelism degree of a training group when a GPU drops out, then uses rack-level dynamic power boosting to overclock the remaining GPUs so a data-parallel replica stays productive, keeping large-scale LLM training Goodput high with under 1% resharding overhead across scale-up domains up to 72 NVLink-connected Blackwell GPUs.

insights.claim: In AI training, Goodput measures useful, convergence-driving work completed rather than raw hardware throughput, making it the metric that determines effective return on large GPU clusters.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating AI infrastructure utilization should track Goodput, not just FLOPS or GPU-hours, since raw throughput overstates productive output during interruptions.
insights.quote: Goodput represents the critical measure of useful, convergence-driving work completed, rather than just raw hardware throughput.
insights.locator: For large-scale training jobs, elastically adapting

insights.claim: As scale-up domains grow from eight to 72 GPUs and beyond, maximizing the productive uptime of every healthy device becomes the key lever for achieving high Goodput.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: Larger NVLink domains raise the blast radius of any single failed GPU, so resilience software becomes a bigger differentiator as domain sizes scale.
insights.locator: As data center architectures evolve to support larger scale-up domains

insights.claim: The longer a training job runs across thousands of GPUs, the greater the likelihood of unscheduled interruptions, and even infrequent device unavailability has outsized effects on tightly interconnected clusters.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: Failure rate scales with cluster size and duration, so frontier training runs face a structural reliability tax that resilience tooling directly addresses.
insights.quote: Even infrequent device unavailability can have outsized effects on tightly interconnected clusters, resulting in slowdowns for a given training run.
insights.locator: Training LLMs at massive scale brings unique infrastructure challenges

insights.claim: Existing elastic-scaling remedies (dropping a data replica, fast checkpoint-restarts, or swapping to hot spares) all incur lost throughput and higher cost while the job runs in a reduced-availability condition.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: Current fault-tolerance approaches leave money on the table during degradation; NTP is positioned to capture that gap, a potential efficiency edge for NVIDIA-stack training.
insights.quote: they also incur some amount of lost throughput and higher cost during the period that the training job is running in a reduced availability condition.
insights.locator: Effective methods for elastic scaling today

insights.claim: Because GPUs in the same tensor-parallel group share tightly coupled computations, a single delayed GPU can slow synchronization across the whole group, causing stalls until the system recovers or redistributes the workload.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: The straggler problem in TP groups is the specific bottleneck NTP targets; understanding it clarifies why per-group elasticity matters more than replica-level fixes.
insights.locator: Training usually involves a pipeline where each stage depends

insights.claim: Nonuniform Tensor Parallelism dynamically adjusts the tensor-parallelism degree in response to transient GPU unavailability and overlaps the necessary data resharding, so a data-parallel replica stays productive rather than being lost during interruptions.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: NTP is NVIDIA's software answer to GPU failures during training; its adoption in Megatron Core signals where hyperscaler training stacks are heading.
insights.quote: NTP’s core contribution is its ability to sustain high Goodput by preventing transient device issues from stalling large, interconnected training jobs.
insights.locator: NTP’s core contribution is its ability to sustain high Goodput

insights.claim: When a GPU drops out, NTP reconfigures the affected group to use only the remaining functional GPUs (e.g., a group of eight switches to a TP degree of seven), and the surviving GPUs each take on increased individual workload to keep the shard computing.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: Graceful degradation to N-1 GPUs instead of losing a replica is the concrete efficiency mechanism; watch for measured Goodput gains versus checkpoint-restart baselines.
insights.quote: if a TP group of eight GPUs experiences one drop-out, it can dynamically switch to a TP degree of seven.

insights.claim: NTP overlaps tensor resharding with the backward computation and parameter synchronization phases, limiting the overhead introduced to healthy replicas to often less than 1%.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: A sub-1% overhead claim is the key performance number to validate; if real, it makes always-on resilience nearly free versus today's costly recovery paths.
insights.quote: the overhead introduced to healthy replicas is minimized, often to less than 1%.

insights.claim: Reducing the TP degree alone hurts throughput because a data-parallel replica with fewer GPUs runs slower and stalls the whole DP system waiting on the slowest replica.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: The slowest-replica constraint is why software elasticity requires a hardware complement; explains the push toward power-boost-capable rack designs.
insights.quote: A DP replica with fewer GPUs will inherently run slower, causing the entire DP system to stall, waiting for the slowest replica.

insights.claim: The study proposes a rack design with improved electrical and thermal capabilities that dynamically raises power to active GPUs in a degraded domain, temporarily elevating clock frequencies so the reduced-TP replica catches up to fully functional replicas.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: Points to next-gen NVIDIA racks with electrical/thermal headroom for dynamic overclocking, a hardware roadmap signal relevant to power, cooling, and rack suppliers.
insights.quote: By dynamically increasing the power supplied to the active GPUs in an affected domain, clock frequencies and computational throughput can be temporarily elevated.

insights.claim: NVIDIA frames NTP as proof that co-designing hardware and software, pairing resilience software with racks that provide electrical and thermal headroom, is essential to unlocking higher performance and steady Goodput in next-generation AI systems.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: Reinforces NVIDIA's full-stack moat argument: value accrues to the integrated hardware+software vendor, not to commodity GPU buyers assembling parts.
insights.quote: This symbiotic relationship between hardware and software is essential for unlocking higher levels of performance, efficiency, steady Goodput and resilience in the next generation of AI systems.
insights.locator: NTP builds a more resilient path to scaled AI training

insights.claim: On NVIDIA Blackwell and Blackwell Ultra systems, NVLink connects up to 72 GPUs at 1,800 GB/s, supporting all-to-all communication within a single hop and defining the scale-up domain that forms a single tensor-parallel group.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: The 72-GPU / 1,800 GB/s NVLink domain is the hardware substrate that makes NTP-style resilience valuable; a concrete spec for sizing Blackwell training economics.
insights.quote: NVLink connects up to 72 GPUs at 1,800 GB/s, supporting all-to-all communication within a single hop.

insights.claim: A single rack of servers forms the scale-up domain and one TP group, while data parallelism replicates the model across multiple such domains, each processing a different batch.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: The rack-equals-TP-group mapping clarifies how NVIDIA is packaging training topology, relevant to how frontier clusters are physically provisioned.
insights.locator: In a typical scenario, a frontier LLM is trained

insights.claim: NTP is a forward-looking experimental feature that has recently been added to the developer branch of NVIDIA Megatron Core, with production fault-tolerance available separately in the NVIDIA Resiliency Extension (NVRx).
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: NTP is experimental, not production, so near-term impact is limited; track its promotion out of the developer branch as the productization signal.

insights.claim: NVIDIA is already extending these resilience concepts to Nonuniform Expert Parallelism (NEP) for Mixture-of-Experts models, where standard tensor parallelism is less ideal.
insights.speaker: Farshad Ghodsian
insights.so_what: Signals NVIDIA is building resilience primitives specifically for MoE architectures, the dominant frontier-model design, extending its software lead into the next model generation.
insights.quote: research is already underway to extend these concepts to Nonuniform Expert Parallelism (NEP), optimizing resilience for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models where standard tensor parallelism is less ideal.
insights.locator: Building on this foundation, research is already underway
